summary:
The Northern and Southern Dynasties (420–589 AD) represent the period of prolonged division in Chinese history following the [[event:2047|Jin dynasty's]] collapse. This entry records the broader political and cultural context of this fragmented era: in the north, the [[event:2048|Northern Wei dynasty]] and its successors (Eastern Wei, Western Wei, Northern Qi, Northern Zhou) governed a territory where Han Chinese and Xianbei nomadic populations intermingled, while in the south the Six Dynasties — Song (420–479), Qi (479–502), Liang (502–557), and Chen (557–589) — maintained Chinese imperial traditions at the court of Jiankang (modern Nanjing).

The cultural legacy of this divided era was paradoxically rich. In the south, aristocratic culture, landscape poetry (taoism-influenced nature poetry), calligraphy, and philosophical debate flourished under the patronage of the southern courts. Tao Yuanming (365–427 AD), considered China's first great nature poet, wrote during this period. In the north, the [[event:2049|spread of Buddhism]] transformed Chinese culture, with the cave temples at Yungang and Longmen representing the period's greatest artistic achievement. The reunification of China under the Sui dynasty in 589 AD, and the subsequent Tang dynasty's golden age, drew on the cultural, religious, and administrative developments of both northern and southern traditions from this period. The scholar-official tradition, the examination system, and the synthesis of Confucianism, Buddhism, and Taoism that characterizes classical Chinese culture all matured during the divisions of the 3rd–6th centuries.
